title: Forcing at the Surface: A Witnessed Introduction

medium.statement: The browser carries bounded pedagogical projections. It is a medium, not shared occupancy.

projection.title: The Forcing Spine
projection.description: Compute the site, forcing relations, generics, and truth on the seven-condition bench.

chapters.title: Conditions and Refinement
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can read the order everything else in this book
computes over: what a condition is, what refinement preserves, why two
commitments can be *incompatible* forever, and what a coherent course of
commitments (a filter) looks like. You will build the **spine model** —
the seven-condition bench the labs of chapters 03–05 run on — and verify
its structure by brute force. You will also learn to hear two words,
*channel* and *forcing*, that mean different things in the paper and in
Sketched, before the collision can hurt you.
chapters.boundary_note:
This chapter does **not** claim that real Sketched sessions satisfy the
displacement budget — the smoothness contract (`asm:smooth`, C) is
measured per substrate, and chapter 06 shows exactly what you are owed
when it holds and how the guarantee dies when it does not. It also does
not claim the spine model *is* the paper's condition poset; it is the
smallest honest bench, and every brute-force **obs** fact above is a
fact about the bench. The general statements live in the paper and, for
the load-bearing ones, in Lean.

sections.title: Aim
sections.markdown:
After this chapter you can read the order everything else in this book
computes over: what a condition is, what refinement preserves, why two
commitments can be *incompatible* forever, and what a coherent course of
commitments (a filter) looks like. You will build the **spine model** —
the seven-condition bench the labs of chapters 03–05 run on — and verify
its structure by brute force. You will also learn to hear two words,
*channel* and *forcing*, that mean different things in the paper and in
Sketched, before the collision can hurt you.

sections.title: Prerequisites
sections.markdown:
Chapter 01 (the void, depth, the wedge). No topology yet; that is
chapter 03.

sections.title: Definitions
sections.markdown:
**Condition** (`def:cond`, D). A finite partial epistemic state. In the
paper: a claim complex with fibers and an exit cone. In Sketched: a
stage — which layers exist, who owns them, what has been consented to so
far. You always know only finitely much; a condition records exactly
that much and no more.

**Refinement and the arena** (`def:refine`, D). `q ⊑ p` iff q is
reachable from p by an admissible move that *retains p's commitments*.
Two consequences, both load-bearing:

- **Work descends.** More knowledge = lower in the order. The root is
  the condition that commits to nothing.
- **The arena is bounded.** All forcing in the paper happens in the
  *channel-margin subposet*: the conditions reachable from the anchor
  within the displacement budget of the smoothness contract
  (`asm:smooth`, C — measured, not assumed). The definition consumes
  only the budget; that the margin actually survives throughout is
  *proved*, not stipulated — chapter 06 is the anatomy of exactly that
  proof.

**Filter** (book-local; the paper uses it from chapter 05 on). A
nonempty set of conditions that is upward closed (whatever you commit
to, you had committed to its weakenings) and downward directed (any two
of its members have a common refinement *inside it*). A filter is a
coherent course of commitments: no member ever contradicts another.

**The spine model.** Conditions `r > {0,1} > {00,01,10,11}` — a depth-2
binary tree. `r` is the root; each address extends the one above it.
This is the smallest model where everything interesting in chapters
03–05 already happens, and the lab builds it from
`verification/kernel/spine.py`.

sections.title: Two words that collide (read this box twice)
sections.markdown:
The paper and Sketched share vocabulary but not referents. The book
always means the paper's sense unless it says otherwise.

- **Channel.** Paper: the *channel-margin subposet* — the region where
  the interaction matrix keeps a positive spectral margin; "the channel
  is open" is a statement about an eigenvalue. Sketched: a *consent
  lease* — a bounded authority a human grants a knob by press-and-hold.
  The two are related by design (the lease is the operational shadow of
  the margin) but they are *not the same object*, and the operator map
  (`docs/13_OPERATOR_MAP.md`) tracks the correspondence explicitly.
- **Forcing.** Paper, two senses it itself separates: a *forced
  transition* (control: the dynamics push a state) versus *p forces φ*
  (logic: the relation this book teaches). Sketched adds a third
  colloquial sense — nobody "forces" a mark onto the shared surface —
  which is precisely what the coordination model forbids. When this
  book says *forces*, it means the logical relation, always.

This box covers the two worst offenders only. The full, machine-checked
account — every double-booked term the book uses, all its senses, and a
verified anchor for each — lives in `book/glossary_collisions.md`
(browser: `/book/glossary`); by the time you reach chapter 08 it also
covers *void* and *surface*, which this book comes to use in more than
one sense of its own.

sections.title: Claims
sections.markdown:
This chapter's claims are structural facts about the spine model, every
one verified by brute force in the lab — status **obs**, the honest tag
for "a machine checked all 128 subsets; nobody wrote a proof."

- Refinement on the spine is a partial order: reflexive, antisymmetric,
  transitive (**obs**).
- `00` and `1` have no common refinement (**obs**). Incompatibility is
  a *permanent* geometric fact — remember it when torsion appears in
  chapter 04.
- The spine has **exactly 7 filters**, one per condition: every filter
  is a principal up-set `up(x)` (**obs**). Coherent histories in a tree
  are just "descend, stopping wherever honesty requires."
- The **4 maximal filters are exactly the branches** — the upward
  closures of the leaves (**obs**). Chapter 05 will call these the
  finite stand-ins for generics.
- No filter contains both `0` and `1` (**obs**): incompatible
  commitments never cohere, which is chapter 01's wedge lemma in
  miniature.

sections.title: Worked example: you never un-know
sections.markdown:
Stabilize atom `a` on the left subtree `{0, 00, 01}` — this is the
valuation chapters 04 and 05 use. It is *refinement-closed*: every
refinement of a condition where a holds is again a condition where a
holds. The kernel's `persistent()` check accepts it.

Now mutate it: let a hold at `0` but *not* at `00` or `01`. The kernel
rejects the valuation, and `Stab(0, a)` flips from true to false — a
condition below which a can be forgotten never stabilized a in the
first place. Persistence is not decoration; it is what makes the
stability set a sieve (chapter 03) and what the ledger tracks as the
paper's highest-leverage modeling postulate (M-Pers). The lab asserts
both directions.

sections.title: Lab
sections.markdown:
```bash
python book/labs/lab02_conditions.py
```

It prints the down-sets, counts the filters by checking all 2⁷
subsets, and asserts every fact quoted above. If the kernel and this
chapter ever disagree, the build fails.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.markdown:
1. **Predict, then run.** How many filters does the depth-3 binary tree
   have? How many are maximal? Write both numbers down; then change
   `binary_tree(2)` to `binary_tree(3)` in a copy of the lab's filter
   count and check.
2. Show by hand that `{r, 0, 1}` is upward closed but not a filter.
   Which pair breaks downward directedness, and why is that the same
   fact as "the wedge has no cross-branch relations"?
3. In a poset that is *not* a tree (draw one with a diamond), exhibit a
   filter that is not principal-along-a-chain. What does Sketched's
   stage graph look like when two consented edits merge?
4. The mutated valuation ("a holds at 0 only") describes a system that
   *revokes* a claim under refinement. Sketched has a legitimate
   operation that looks like forgetting — shake. Say precisely why
   shake is not a counterexample to persistence. (Hint: chapter 01's
   depth-0; a fresh context window is a new descent, not a move within
   the old one.)

sections.title: Boundary note
sections.markdown:
This chapter does **not** claim that real Sketched sessions satisfy the
displacement budget — the smoothness contract (`asm:smooth`, C) is
measured per substrate, and chapter 06 shows exactly what you are owed
when it holds and how the guarantee dies when it does not. It also does
not claim the spine model *is* the paper's condition poset; it is the
smallest honest bench, and every brute-force **obs** fact above is a
fact about the bench. The general statements live in the paper and, for
the load-bearing ones, in Lean.

chapters.raw_markdown:
# Chapter 02 · Conditions and Refinement

## Aim

After this chapter you can read the order everything else in this book
computes over: what a condition is, what refinement preserves, why two
commitments can be *incompatible* forever, and what a coherent course of
commitments (a filter) looks like. You will build the **spine model** —
the seven-condition bench the labs of chapters 03–05 run on — and verify
its structure by brute force. You will also learn to hear two words,
*channel* and *forcing*, that mean different things in the paper and in
Sketched, before the collision can hurt you.

## Prerequisites

Chapter 01 (the void, depth, the wedge). No topology yet; that is
chapter 03.

## Definitions

**Condition** (`def:cond`, D). A finite partial epistemic state. In the
paper: a claim complex with fibers and an exit cone. In Sketched: a
stage — which layers exist, who owns them, what has been consented to so
far. You always know only finitely much; a condition records exactly
that much and no more.

**Refinement and the arena** (`def:refine`, D). `q ⊑ p` iff q is
reachable from p by an admissible move that *retains p's commitments*.
Two consequences, both load-bearing:

- **Work descends.** More knowledge = lower in the order. The root is
  the condition that commits to nothing.
- **The arena is bounded.** All forcing in the paper happens in the
  *channel-margin subposet*: the conditions reachable from the anchor
  within the displacement budget of the smoothness contract
  (`asm:smooth`, C — measured, not assumed). The definition consumes
  only the budget; that the margin actually survives throughout is
  *proved*, not stipulated — chapter 06 is the anatomy of exactly that
  proof.

**Filter** (book-local; the paper uses it from chapter 05 on). A
nonempty set of conditions that is upward closed (whatever you commit
to, you had committed to its weakenings) and downward directed (any two
of its members have a common refinement *inside it*). A filter is a
coherent course of commitments: no member ever contradicts another.

**The spine model.** Conditions `r > {0,1} > {00,01,10,11}` — a depth-2
binary tree. `r` is the root; each address extends the one above it.
This is the smallest model where everything interesting in chapters
03–05 already happens, and the lab builds it from
`verification/kernel/spine.py`.

## Two words that collide (read this box twice)

The paper and Sketched share vocabulary but not referents. The book
always means the paper's sense unless it says otherwise.

- **Channel.** Paper: the *channel-margin subposet* — the region where
  the interaction matrix keeps a positive spectral margin; "the channel
  is open" is a statement about an eigenvalue. Sketched: a *consent
  lease* — a bounded authority a human grants a knob by press-and-hold.
  The two are related by design (the lease is the operational shadow of
  the margin) but they are *not the same object*, and the operator map
  (`docs/13_OPERATOR_MAP.md`) tracks the correspondence explicitly.
- **Forcing.** Paper, two senses it itself separates: a *forced
  transition* (control: the dynamics push a state) versus *p forces φ*
  (logic: the relation this book teaches). Sketched adds a third
  colloquial sense — nobody "forces" a mark onto the shared surface —
  which is precisely what the coordination model forbids. When this
  book says *forces*, it means the logical relation, always.

This box covers the two worst offenders only. The full, machine-checked
account — every double-booked term the book uses, all its senses, and a
verified anchor for each — lives in `book/glossary_collisions.md`
(browser: `/book/glossary`); by the time you reach chapter 08 it also
covers *void* and *surface*, which this book comes to use in more than
one sense of its own.

## Claims

This chapter's claims are structural facts about the spine model, every
one verified by brute force in the lab — status **obs**, the honest tag
for "a machine checked all 128 subsets; nobody wrote a proof."

- Refinement on the spine is a partial order: reflexive, antisymmetric,
  transitive (**obs**).
- `00` and `1` have no common refinement (**obs**). Incompatibility is
  a *permanent* geometric fact — remember it when torsion appears in
  chapter 04.
- The spine has **exactly 7 filters**, one per condition: every filter
  is a principal up-set `up(x)` (**obs**). Coherent histories in a tree
  are just "descend, stopping wherever honesty requires."
- The **4 maximal filters are exactly the branches** — the upward
  closures of the leaves (**obs**). Chapter 05 will call these the
  finite stand-ins for generics.
- No filter contains both `0` and `1` (**obs**): incompatible
  commitments never cohere, which is chapter 01's wedge lemma in
  miniature.

## Worked example: you never un-know

Stabilize atom `a` on the left subtree `{0, 00, 01}` — this is the
valuation chapters 04 and 05 use. It is *refinement-closed*: every
refinement of a condition where a holds is again a condition where a
holds. The kernel's `persistent()` check accepts it.

Now mutate it: let a hold at `0` but *not* at `00` or `01`. The kernel
rejects the valuation, and `Stab(0, a)` flips from true to false — a
condition below which a can be forgotten never stabilized a in the
first place. Persistence is not decoration; it is what makes the
stability set a sieve (chapter 03) and what the ledger tracks as the
paper's highest-leverage modeling postulate (M-Pers). The lab asserts
both directions.

## Lab

```bash
python book/labs/lab02_conditions.py
```

It prints the down-sets, counts the filters by checking all 2⁷
subsets, and asserts every fact quoted above. If the kernel and this
chapter ever disagree, the build fails.

## Exercises

1. **Predict, then run.** How many filters does the depth-3 binary tree
   have? How many are maximal? Write both numbers down; then change
   `binary_tree(2)` to `binary_tree(3)` in a copy of the lab's filter
   count and check.
2. Show by hand that `{r, 0, 1}` is upward closed but not a filter.
   Which pair breaks downward directedness, and why is that the same
   fact as "the wedge has no cross-branch relations"?
3. In a poset that is *not* a tree (draw one with a diamond), exhibit a
   filter that is not principal-along-a-chain. What does Sketched's
   stage graph look like when two consented edits merge?
4. The mutated valuation ("a holds at 0 only") describes a system that
   *revokes* a claim under refinement. Sketched has a legitimate
   operation that looks like forgetting — shake. Say precisely why
   shake is not a counterexample to persistence. (Hint: chapter 01's
   depth-0; a fresh context window is a new descent, not a move within
   the old one.)

## Boundary note

This chapter does **not** claim that real Sketched sessions satisfy the
displacement budget — the smoothness contract (`asm:smooth`, C) is
measured per substrate, and chapter 06 shows exactly what you are owed
when it holds and how the guarantee dies when it does not. It also does
not claim the spine model *is* the paper's condition poset; it is the
smallest honest bench, and every brute-force **obs** fact above is a
fact about the bench. The general statements live in the paper and, for
the load-bearing ones, in Lean.

dependents.title: Forcing: When Does a Claim Hold?
The Margin: Anatomy of a Repair

chapters.title: Sieves, Covers, and Two Topologies
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can say what a *cover* is on the spine model, name
the two covering rules the rest of this book distinguishes everywhere,
and prove — by counting, not by trusting the diagram — that one is
strictly finer than the other. This is the last piece of scaffolding
before chapter 04 lets you compute forcing by hand.
chapters.boundary_note:
This chapter does **not** claim `J_adm` is the topology real Sketched
sessions use — the spine's one generator is a minimal illustration, not
the operational admissibility class. It also does not claim strictness
is a defect: `J_adm ⊊ J_nn` is exactly what lets the calibrated reading
refuse to certify a decision that mere density would wave through. What
is claimed: both are genuine sites on this model (Grothendieck axioms
checked, not assumed), the Site Bound is machine-verified, and the
witness of strictness is a fact about the computed sieve lattice, not a
picked example.

sections.title: Aim
sections.markdown:
After this chapter you can say what a *cover* is on the spine model, name
the two covering rules the rest of this book distinguishes everywhere,
and prove — by counting, not by trusting the diagram — that one is
strictly finer than the other. This is the last piece of scaffolding
before chapter 04 lets you compute forcing by hand.

sections.title: Prerequisites
sections.markdown: Chapter 02 (conditions, refinement, the spine model, filters).

sections.title: Definitions
sections.markdown:
**Sieve** (part of `def:req`, D). A downward-closed subset `S ⊆ down(p)`:
if `q ∈ S` and `s ⊑ q`, then `s ∈ S`. A sieve is a *request that, once
granted below some point, stays granted below everything finer* — the
same closure filters already have, but a sieve need not be directed or
even connected; it just cannot un-grant. On the spine, `down(r)` has 26
sieves (every down-closed subset of 7 elements); a leaf has exactly 2 —
empty, and everything (itself).

**Admissible dense requirement** (`def:req`, D). A family D below p is
*admissible dense* if every refinement of p has a further *admissible*
refinement meeting D. Not every dense family qualifies — density alone
is cheap; admissibility is the operational cost. D generates the sieve
`S_D = {q ⊑ p : some admissible refinement of q meets D}`.

**The two topologies.**

- **`J_nn`, the dense (double-negation) topology.** `S` covers `p` iff
  `S` is dense below `p`: every `q ⊑ p` has some `r ⊑ q` in `S`. This is
  the ambient reading — it asks only "is the evidence out there
  somewhere," with no cost accounting.
- **Generated admissible site `J_adm`** (`def:site`, D). Let
  `B = {S_D : D admissible dense}`. `J_adm = ⟨B⟩`, the *least*
  Grothendieck topology containing `B` — closed under pullback and
  local character, nothing more. On the spine, `J_adm` is generated by
  one requirement on `r`: "leave the void" (every non-root condition
  covers `r`).

A Grothendieck topology needs three things at every condition: the
maximal sieve always covers, covers pull back along refinement, and
covers can be verified locally (if a cover-of-covers is really a cover,
it's already in). The lab checks the first two directly for both `J_nn`
and `J_adm`; both hold at all 7 conditions of the spine.

sections.title: Claims
sections.markdown:
**Site Bound** (`lem:sitebound`, **P**, machine-verified). `J_adm ⊆ J_nn`
at every condition: every admissibly-generated cover is dense, but not
every dense sieve is admissibly generated. Proof idea (the paper gives
the full argument): `J_nn` is *itself* a Grothendieck topology on any
poset, each generator `S_D` is dense by definition of admissible
density, so the *least* topology containing the generators sits inside
it.

**The containment is strict — the lab exhibits the witness.** At
`p = r`, the leaf sieve `S = {00, 01, 10, 11}` is `J_nn`-dense (every
condition below r has a leaf below it) but is **not** a `J_adm`-cover:
the only `J_adm` generator on r is "leave the void," and closing it
under pullback and local character never manufactures the leaf sieve.
Density says the evidence exists somewhere in the future; `J_adm` asks
whether the *specific admissible moves you were actually granted* reach
it. Chapter 04 names the gap between these two readings **Decision
Reachability** — this chapter proves the gap is nonempty before that
name is needed.

sections.title: Worked example: what "leave the void" buys, and what it doesn't
sections.markdown:
The single generator on r says: every non-root condition together forms
an admissible cover of r. That's cheap — it only asks you to leave depth
0 at all. It buys `r ⊩_adm a` nowhere by itself; what it buys is a
*foothold*: `J_adm` at `0` and `1` still has to be built up from
whatever admissible requirements exist *there*. On the 7-condition
spine, with no further generators declared, `J_adm` below `0` and `1`
only contains their own maximal sieves — nothing more is admissibly
certified. That asymmetry (rich at r's one generator, empty below it) is
not a limitation of this toy model; it is the calibration queue in
miniature — chapter 08 names it `asm:calibration` and treats it as
honest open debt, not a bug to be quietly patched.

sections.title: Lab
sections.markdown:
```bash
python book/labs/lab03_topologies.py
```

It enumerates all sieves on all 7 conditions, builds `J_nn` and `J_adm`,
checks the Grothendieck axioms hold for both, verifies the Site Bound by
brute force over every sieve at every condition, and prints the specific
strictness witness quoted above.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.markdown:
1. **Predict, then run.** Add a second `J_adm` generator: "leave 0" (all
   of `{00, 01}` covers `0`). Does `r ⊩_adm b` (b = all leaves) become
   true? Predict using the pullback-stability requirement, then patch
   the lab's generator dict and check.
2. Count the sieves on `0` by hand (5, per the lab) and list them. Which
   one is the maximal sieve, and which is `A_a(0)` from chapter 02's
   persistent valuation?
3. Prove `J_nn` is a Grothendieck topology on an arbitrary finite poset
   in three lines, using only that `⊑` is transitive.
4. (Harder.) The witness sieve `{00,01,10,11}` fails to be a `J_adm`
   cover of `r` for a *structural* reason, not an accident of this
   generator choice: exhibit a second, different single-generator choice
   on `r` under which the *same* sieve still fails to be a cover, and
   say what property of "leave the void"-style generators makes that
   inevitable.

sections.title: Boundary note
sections.markdown:
This chapter does **not** claim `J_adm` is the topology real Sketched
sessions use — the spine's one generator is a minimal illustration, not
the operational admissibility class. It also does not claim strictness
is a defect: `J_adm ⊊ J_nn` is exactly what lets the calibrated reading
refuse to certify a decision that mere density would wave through. What
is claimed: both are genuine sites on this model (Grothendieck axioms
checked, not assumed), the Site Bound is machine-verified, and the
witness of strictness is a fact about the computed sieve lattice, not a
picked example.

chapters.raw_markdown:
# Chapter 03 · Sieves, Covers, and Two Topologies

## Aim

After this chapter you can say what a *cover* is on the spine model, name
the two covering rules the rest of this book distinguishes everywhere,
and prove — by counting, not by trusting the diagram — that one is
strictly finer than the other. This is the last piece of scaffolding
before chapter 04 lets you compute forcing by hand.

## Prerequisites

Chapter 02 (conditions, refinement, the spine model, filters).

## Definitions

**Sieve** (part of `def:req`, D). A downward-closed subset `S ⊆ down(p)`:
if `q ∈ S` and `s ⊑ q`, then `s ∈ S`. A sieve is a *request that, once
granted below some point, stays granted below everything finer* — the
same closure filters already have, but a sieve need not be directed or
even connected; it just cannot un-grant. On the spine, `down(r)` has 26
sieves (every down-closed subset of 7 elements); a leaf has exactly 2 —
empty, and everything (itself).

**Admissible dense requirement** (`def:req`, D). A family D below p is

refinement meeting D. Not every dense family qualifies — density alone
is cheap; admissibility is the operational cost. D generates the sieve

**The two topologies.**

- **`J_nn`, the dense (double-negation) topology.** `S` covers `p` iff

  the ambient reading — it asks only "is the evidence out there
  somewhere," with no cost accounting.
- **Generated admissible site `J_adm`** (`def:site`, D). Let
  `B = {S_D : D admissible dense}`. `J_adm = ⟨B⟩`, the *least*

  local character, nothing more. On the spine, `J_adm` is generated by

A Grothendieck topology needs three things at every condition: the
maximal sieve always covers, covers pull back along refinement, and
covers can be verified locally (if a cover-of-covers is really a cover,

## Claims

**Site Bound** (`lem:sitebound`, **P**, machine-verified). `J_adm ⊆ J_nn`
at every condition: every admissibly-generated cover is dense, but not
every dense sieve is admissibly generated. Proof idea (the paper gives

**The containment is strict — the lab exhibits the witness.** At
`p = r`, the leaf sieve `S = {00, 01, 10, 11}` is `J_nn`-dense (every
condition below r has a leaf below it) but is **not** a `J_adm`-cover:

whether the *specific admissible moves you were actually granted* reach
it. Chapter 04 names the gap between these two readings **Decision

name is needed.

## Worked example: what "leave the void" buys, and what it doesn't

The single generator on r says: every non-root condition together forms

0 at all. It buys `r ⊩_adm a` nowhere by itself; what it buys is a
*foothold*: `J_adm` at `0` and `1` still has to be built up from
whatever admissible requirements exist *there*. On the 7-condition
spine, with no further generators declared, `J_adm` below `0` and `1`
only contains their own maximal sieves — nothing more is admissibly
certified. That asymmetry (rich at r's one generator, empty below it) is
not a limitation of this toy model; it is the calibration queue in
miniature — chapter 08 names it `asm:calibration` and treats it as
honest open debt, not a bug to be quietly patched.

## Lab

```bash
python book/labs/lab03_topologies.py
```

It enumerates all sieves on all 7 conditions, builds `J_nn` and `J_adm`,
checks the Grothendieck axioms hold for both, verifies the Site Bound by
brute force over every sieve at every condition, and prints the specific
strictness witness quoted above.

## Exercises

1. **Predict, then run.** Add a second `J_adm` generator: "leave 0" (all
   of `{00, 01}` covers `0`). Does `r ⊩_adm b` (b = all leaves) become
   true? Predict using the pullback-stability requirement, then patch
   the lab's generator dict and check.
2. Count the sieves on `0` by hand (5, per the lab) and list them. Which
   one is the maximal sieve, and which is `A_a(0)` from chapter 02's
   persistent valuation?
3. Prove `J_nn` is a Grothendieck topology on an arbitrary finite poset
   in three lines, using only that `⊑` is transitive.
4. (Harder.) The witness sieve `{00,01,10,11}` fails to be a `J_adm`
   cover of `r` for a *structural* reason, not an accident of this
   generator choice: exhibit a second, different single-generator choice
   on `r` under which the *same* sieve still fails to be a cover, and
   say what property of "leave the void"-style generators makes that
   inevitable.

## Boundary note

This chapter does **not** claim `J_adm` is the topology real Sketched
sessions use — the spine's one generator is a minimal illustration, not
the operational admissibility class. It also does not claim strictness
is a defect: `J_adm ⊊ J_nn` is exactly what lets the calibrated reading
refuse to certify a decision that mere density would wave through. What
is claimed: both are genuine sites on this model (Grothendieck axioms
checked, not assumed), the Site Bound is machine-verified, and the
witness of strictness is a fact about the computed sieve lattice, not a
picked example.

chapters.title: Forcing: When Does a Claim Hold?
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can compute, by hand on a seven-condition model,
whether a condition *forces* a claim — under both of the system's
readings — and you can explain the three distinctions everything else in
this book leans on: forced vs. true-here, undecided vs. refuted, and
contingent refusal vs. torsion. You will also meet the first place the
two readings genuinely disagree, and why that disagreement is a feature
with a name (Decision Reachability), not a bug.
chapters.boundary_note:
This chapter does **not** claim that Sketched's consent pipeline
*implements* these clauses — that is the calibration queue
(`asm:calibration`), and it is open. What is claimed: the clauses are
machine-verified mathematics (`lem:pers`, `lem:dec`, truth lemma in
chapter 05), the model is executable, and the analogy is tracked

sections.title: Aim
sections.markdown:
After this chapter you can compute, by hand on a seven-condition model,
whether a condition *forces* a claim — under both of the system's
readings — and you can explain the three distinctions everything else in
this book leans on: forced vs. true-here, undecided vs. refuted, and
contingent refusal vs. torsion. You will also meet the first place the
two readings genuinely disagree, and why that disagreement is a feature
with a name (Decision Reachability), not a bug.

sections.title: Prerequisites
sections.markdown:
Chapters 01–03 (conditions, refinement, sieves, the two topologies
J_nn ⊇ J_adm and the Site Bound `lem:sitebound`). All computations here
use the **spine model**: conditions `r > {0,1} > {00,01,10,11}` (a
depth-2 binary tree), atom `a` stabilized on the left subtree
`{0,00,01}`, atom `b` stabilized on the leaves, and J_adm generated by
one admissible requirement on `r` — "leave the void."

sections.title: Definitions
sections.markdown:
**Stability, atomic** (`def:stab`, D). `Stab(q, a)`: the atom holds at q
and persists under every further refinement. The stability set
`A_a(p) = {q ⊑ p : Stab(q, a)}` is a sieve — this is where the
persistence postulate (M-Pers) is consumed, and the ledger says so.

**Forcing** (`def:force`, D). Two relations, never silently conflated:

- `p ⊩_H a` — the **calibrated** reading: `A_a(p)` is a J_adm-cover;
- `p ⊩_nn a` — the **dense** reading: `A_a(p)` is dense below p.

By the Site Bound, calibrated ⇒ dense. The converse is the whole drama.

**The clauses** (`def:clauses`, D). `∧` componentwise; `∨` when the
disjunct-forcers form a cover; and the load-bearing one:

> `p ⊩ ¬φ` iff **no** refinement of p forces φ.

Negation quantifies over the entire future. Hold that thought.

sections.title: Claims
sections.markdown:
**Persistence and consistency** (`lem:pers`, **P**, machine-verified).
Whatever p forces, every refinement of p forces; and nothing forces both
φ and ¬φ. Forcing is a promise that survives more knowledge.

**Deciding sets are order-dense** (`lem:dec`, **P**, machine-verified).
Below any condition there is always a refinement that decides φ, one way
or the other. Density in the *order* is free; whether the deciding set is
an *admissible* cover is Decision Reachability (`lem:reach`, P consuming
the smoothness contract) — chapter 05's hinge.

**Torsion is the atomic case only** (`rem:torsion`, **P** atomic /
**O** compound). "Refuted at every refinement" and "no refinement can
force it" coincide for atoms and come apart for compounds — the lab
shows the countermodel live.

sections.title: Worked example: reading the tables
sections.markdown:
Run the lab; it prints these (x = forces):

```
   ||-_nn        a    b   ~a   ~b  ~~b        ||-_adm       a    b   ~a   ~b  ~~b
      r          .    x    .    .    x           r          .    .    .    .    x
      0          x    x    .    .    x           0          x    .    .    .    x
      1          .    x    x    .    x           1          .    .    x    .    x
   leaves 00,01  x    x    .    .    x        leaves 00,01  x    x    .    .    x
   leaves 10,11  .    x    x    .    x        leaves 10,11  .    x    x    .    x
```

Five readings, in increasing order of consequence:

1. **Forced ≠ true everywhere.** `0 ⊩ a` because a is stable on 0's
   entire cone. But `r ⊮ a`: below condition 1 nothing stabilizes a, so
   the stabilizers are not dense below r. At r, the claim a is simply
   not settled — and the system says so instead of guessing.

2. **Undecided ≠ refuted.** `r ⊮ a` *and* `r ⊮ ¬a` (condition 0 below r
   forces a, so the ¬-clause fails too). r is **undecided** on a. The
   deciding set D_a is everything except r — order-dense, exactly as
   `lem:dec` promises. Decisions live down the branches; bivalence is a
   property of completed journeys, not of waypoints.

3. **Torsion.** `1 ⊩ ¬a`: no refinement of 1 will *ever* stabilize a.
   That is not "denied for now"; it is structural — a refusal stable
   under the entire future. Sketched's human-layer guard is exactly this
   shape: "consent cannot be granted" is a torsion certificate, not a
   pending request. Contingent and permanent refusal are different
   mathematical objects, and the consent layer inherits the distinction.

4. **The two readings disagree — strictness, live.** `r ⊩_nn b` (every
   condition has a leaf below it, and leaves stabilize b) but
   `r ⊮_adm b`: the leaf sieve is dense yet is *not* a J_adm-cover of r,
   because our austere generator never authorized it. Density says the
   evidence is out there; the calibrated reading asks whether *admissible
   moves can reach it*. The gap between the columns is Decision
   Reachability made visible — and note how much more the calibrated
   table withholds: even `0 ⊮_adm b`.

5. **Compound negation misbehaves.** `r ⊩ ¬¬b` by the clause — nobody
   can ever force ¬b, since every condition has a b-stabilizing leaf
   below it. But "¬b is classically stable everywhere below r" is
   *false* — at every leaf, b holds. For the compound ¬b, "no one can
   force it" and "it fails stably everywhere" diverge. For atoms they
   coincide (`rem:torsion`); the paper marks the compound case **O**,
   and the finite model checker found this exact countermodel
   (φ = ¬b at r) during the audit that produced v15.

sections.title: Lab
sections.markdown:
```bash
python book/labs/lab04_forcing.py
```

Every fact quoted above is an assertion in the lab. If the kernel and
this chapter ever disagree, the build fails — the prose is not allowed
to outlive its evidence.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.markdown:
1. **Predict, then run.** Add atom `c` stabilized only at `{01}` to a
   copy of the lab. Which conditions force c under ⊩_nn? Which force ¬c?
   Write the eight-row column first; then run.
2. From the table: which conditions decide *both* a and b under ⊩_nn?
   What is special about them? (Chapter 05 will name it.)
3. Show by hand that `D_a` restricted below r is a sieve (you need
   persistence — say where).
4. Enlarge the J_adm generators by one sieve so that `r ⊩_adm b`
   becomes true, without violating the Site Bound. What did you just
   "spend" to buy that decision? (This is the calibration queue in
   miniature.)
5. (Harder.) Construct a formula and a condition where ⊩_adm and ⊩_nn
   agree at every *leaf* but disagree at an interior node. Why can this
   never happen at leaves in a finite model?

sections.title: Boundary note
sections.markdown:
This chapter does **not** claim that Sketched's consent pipeline
*implements* these clauses — that is the calibration queue
(`asm:calibration`), and it is open. What is claimed: the clauses are
machine-verified mathematics (`lem:pers`, `lem:dec`, truth lemma in
chapter 05), the model is executable, and the analogy is tracked
operator-by-operator in `docs/13_OPERATOR_MAP.md` with realized/target
statuses the build re-checks. Interpretation is free; conflation is not.

chapters.raw_markdown:
# Chapter 04 · Forcing: When Does a Claim Hold?

## Aim

After this chapter you can compute, by hand on a seven-condition model,
whether a condition *forces* a claim — under both of the system's
readings — and you can explain the three distinctions everything else in
this book leans on: forced vs. true-here, undecided vs. refuted, and
contingent refusal vs. torsion. You will also meet the first place the
two readings genuinely disagree, and why that disagreement is a feature
with a name (Decision Reachability), not a bug.

## Prerequisites

Chapters 01–03 (conditions, refinement, sieves, the two topologies
J_nn ⊇ J_adm and the Site Bound `lem:sitebound`). All computations here
use the **spine model**: conditions `r > {0,1} > {00,01,10,11}` (a
depth-2 binary tree), atom `a` stabilized on the left subtree
`{0,00,01}`, atom `b` stabilized on the leaves, and J_adm generated by
one admissible requirement on `r` — "leave the void."

## Definitions

**Stability, atomic** (`def:stab`, D). `Stab(q, a)`: the atom holds at q
and persists under every further refinement. The stability set
`A_a(p) = {q ⊑ p : Stab(q, a)}` is a sieve — this is where the
persistence postulate (M-Pers) is consumed, and the ledger says so.

**Forcing** (`def:force`, D). Two relations, never silently conflated:

- `p ⊩_H a` — the **calibrated** reading: `A_a(p)` is a J_adm-cover;
- `p ⊩_nn a` — the **dense** reading: `A_a(p)` is dense below p.

By the Site Bound, calibrated ⇒ dense. The converse is the whole drama.

**The clauses** (`def:clauses`, D). `∧` componentwise; `∨` when the
disjunct-forcers form a cover; and the load-bearing one:

> `p ⊩ ¬φ` iff **no** refinement of p forces φ.

Negation quantifies over the entire future. Hold that thought.

## Claims

**Persistence and consistency** (`lem:pers`, **P**, machine-verified).
Whatever p forces, every refinement of p forces; and nothing forces both
φ and ¬φ. Forcing is a promise that survives more knowledge.

**Deciding sets are order-dense** (`lem:dec`, **P**, machine-verified).
Below any condition there is always a refinement that decides φ, one way
or the other. Density in the *order* is free; whether the deciding set is
an *admissible* cover is Decision Reachability (`lem:reach`, P consuming
the smoothness contract) — chapter 05's hinge.

**Torsion is the atomic case only** (`rem:torsion`, **P** atomic /
**O** compound). "Refuted at every refinement" and "no refinement can
force it" coincide for atoms and come apart for compounds — the lab
shows the countermodel live.

## Worked example: reading the tables

Run the lab; it prints these (x = forces):

```
   ||-_nn        a    b   ~a   ~b  ~~b        ||-_adm       a    b   ~a   ~b  ~~b
      r          .    x    .    .    x           r          .    .    .    .    x
      0          x    x    .    .    x           0          x    .    .    .    x
      1          .    x    x    .    x           1          .    .    x    .    x
   leaves 00,01  x    x    .    .    x        leaves 00,01  x    x    .    .    x
   leaves 10,11  .    x    x    .    x        leaves 10,11  .    x    x    .    x
```

Five readings, in increasing order of consequence:

1. **Forced ≠ true everywhere.** `0 ⊩ a` because a is stable on 0's
   entire cone. But `r ⊮ a`: below condition 1 nothing stabilizes a, so
   the stabilizers are not dense below r. At r, the claim a is simply
   not settled — and the system says so instead of guessing.

2. **Undecided ≠ refuted.** `r ⊮ a` *and* `r ⊮ ¬a` (condition 0 below r
   forces a, so the ¬-clause fails too). r is **undecided** on a. The
   deciding set D_a is everything except r — order-dense, exactly as
   `lem:dec` promises. Decisions live down the branches; bivalence is a
   property of completed journeys, not of waypoints.

3. **Torsion.** `1 ⊩ ¬a`: no refinement of 1 will *ever* stabilize a.
   That is not "denied for now"; it is structural — a refusal stable
   under the entire future. Sketched's human-layer guard is exactly this
   shape: "consent cannot be granted" is a torsion certificate, not a
   pending request. Contingent and permanent refusal are different
   mathematical objects, and the consent layer inherits the distinction.

4. **The two readings disagree — strictness, live.** `r ⊩_nn b` (every
   condition has a leaf below it, and leaves stabilize b) but
   `r ⊮_adm b`: the leaf sieve is dense yet is *not* a J_adm-cover of r,
   because our austere generator never authorized it. Density says the
   evidence is out there; the calibrated reading asks whether *admissible
   moves can reach it*. The gap between the columns is Decision
   Reachability made visible — and note how much more the calibrated
   table withholds: even `0 ⊮_adm b`.

5. **Compound negation misbehaves.** `r ⊩ ¬¬b` by the clause — nobody
   can ever force ¬b, since every condition has a b-stabilizing leaf
   below it. But "¬b is classically stable everywhere below r" is
   *false* — at every leaf, b holds. For the compound ¬b, "no one can
   force it" and "it fails stably everywhere" diverge. For atoms they
   coincide (`rem:torsion`); the paper marks the compound case **O**,
   and the finite model checker found this exact countermodel
   (φ = ¬b at r) during the audit that produced v15.

## Lab

```bash
python book/labs/lab04_forcing.py
```

Every fact quoted above is an assertion in the lab. If the kernel and
this chapter ever disagree, the build fails — the prose is not allowed
to outlive its evidence.

## Exercises

1. **Predict, then run.** Add atom `c` stabilized only at `{01}` to a
   copy of the lab. Which conditions force c under ⊩_nn? Which force ¬c?
   Write the eight-row column first; then run.
2. From the table: which conditions decide *both* a and b under ⊩_nn?
   What is special about them? (Chapter 05 will name it.)
3. Show by hand that `D_a` restricted below r is a sieve (you need
   persistence — say where).
4. Enlarge the J_adm generators by one sieve so that `r ⊩_adm b`
   becomes true, without violating the Site Bound. What did you just
   "spend" to buy that decision? (This is the calibration queue in
   miniature.)
5. (Harder.) Construct a formula and a condition where ⊩_adm and ⊩_nn
   agree at every *leaf* but disagree at an interior node. Why can this
   never happen at leaves in a finite model?

## Boundary note

This chapter does **not** claim that Sketched's consent pipeline
*implements* these clauses — that is the calibration queue
(`asm:calibration`), and it is open. What is claimed: the clauses are
machine-verified mathematics (`lem:pers`, `lem:dec`, truth lemma in
chapter 05), the model is executable, and the analogy is tracked
operator-by-operator in `docs/13_OPERATOR_MAP.md` with realized/target
statuses the build re-checks. Interpretation is free; conflation is not.

chapters.title: Generics and Truth
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can state Rasiowa–Sikorski genericity, say what
Decision Reachability adds beyond plain order-density, and explain why a
generic *decides every formula* (bivalence) without that fact being
magic. You will also learn this chapter's hardest lesson: the spine
model is small enough to compute on and too small to show you the
interesting part. Naming that gap honestly is the chapter's real
content.
chapters.boundary_note:
This chapter does **not** claim the lab demonstrates Rasiowa–Sikorski
constructing anything, or that Decision Reachability's conditionality is
exercised here — both require either a genuinely infinite dense family
or a site where the smoothness contract actually fails, and the
7-condition spine has neither. What the lab *does* verify: the
truth-lemma pattern holds exactly where the paper says it must, and the
undecided-root/decided-branch asymmetry that motivates the whole
apparatus is a real, computed fact, not a story. The substantive proofs
— that the construction terminates for genuinely unbounded families, and
what happens when the contract is violated — live in the paper's proof
environments and, for the base cases, in the Lean kernel
(`rasiowa_sikorski`, `truth_lemma`, `exists_generic_truth`); this chapter
is not a substitute for either.

sections.title: Aim
sections.markdown:
After this chapter you can state Rasiowa–Sikorski genericity, say what
Decision Reachability adds beyond plain order-density, and explain why a
generic *decides every formula* (bivalence) without that fact being
magic. You will also learn this chapter's hardest lesson: the spine
model is small enough to compute on and too small to show you the
interesting part. Naming that gap honestly is the chapter's real
content.

sections.title: Prerequisites
sections.markdown:
Chapters 02–04 (conditions, the two topologies, forcing, torsion,
Decision Reachability introduced informally). This chapter states the
lemma that names it.

sections.title: Definitions
sections.markdown:
**Generic (finite stand-in).** A filter `G` through `p` meeting every
admissible dense requirement. On the spine, the four **branches** — root-
to-leaf chains — are the finite stand-ins: total, maximal, decided at
every leaf by construction.

**Admissible deciding closure** `D_p*` (`def:closure`, D). The countable
family obtained by adding every atomic stability set and every
propositional deciding set to `D_p`, keeping only those that are
`J_adm`-covers (which Decision Reachability, below, says exist). A
generic meeting `D_p*` is what "decides every formula" means precisely.

sections.title: Claims
sections.markdown:
**Boundedness yields countability for Rasiowa–Sikorski** (`lem:bdd`,
**P**, machine-verified — `rasiowa_sikorski` in the Lean kernel). A
resource-bounded observer tracks only countably many claims over a run
(the observer-bound postulate M-Bound supplies *countability*, not
density); Rasiowa–Sikorski applied to a countable family of dense sets
produces a filter meeting all of them. This is where the classical
forcing machinery — built for countably infinite dense sets — actually
does work: the theorem is only interesting when the family is genuinely
unbounded, not when it has seven elements.

**Decision Reachability, conditional** (`lem:reach`, **P** consuming the
smoothness contract). Under the smoothness contract (`asm:smooth`, C),
every condition has an admissible refinement deciding any given formula
— equivalently, the deciding sieve is not just order-dense but an actual
`J_adm`-cover. This is *not* free: order-density (`lem:dec`, chapter 04)
holds unconditionally, but admissible density is the contract's payoff,
and where the contract fails, the finite model checker exhibits sites
where it does fail. The step from order-dense to admissibly-dense **is**
the calibration debt, not a consequence of it.

**Genericity decides every formula** (`lem:bivalence`, **P** consuming
C). A generic meeting `D_p*` decides every propositional formula — some
condition in it forces φ or forces ¬φ. Bivalence on the generic is P
*conditional on Decision Reachability*, not on countability alone.

**Propositional Truth Lemma, conditional on calibration** (`thm:prop`,
**P** — `truth_lemma`/`exists_generic_truth` in the Lean kernel, under
the calibration queue `asm:calibration`). For a generic G deciding every
formula: `G ⊨_H φ ⟺ ∃p ∈ G : p ⊩_H φ`. Induction on φ; the atomic base
case is `lem:atomic`, whose (⇒) direction routes through the *maximal
sieve* — the v14 text's mistake was concluding this from density alone,
which does not imply `J_adm`-covering; only the maximal sieve, present
in every Grothendieck topology, does.

sections.title: Worked example: the truth lemma pattern, live — and its trivialization
sections.markdown:
The lab checks, over all 4 branches and 7 formulas (a, b, ¬a, ¬b, ¬¬b,
a∧b, a∨b): **`G ⊨ φ` iff some `q ∈ G` forces φ under `⊩_adm`.** Every one
of the 28 checks matches. That is a genuine, computed instance of
`thm:prop`'s pattern — not asserted, verified.

Now the honest part. Every branch on this model **decides every
formula, trivially** — bivalence never fails, in fact *cannot* fail,
because the valuation is total on the leaves: a leaf is either in `V(a)`
or it isn't, full stop, and a branch's classical truth is read straight
off its leaf. That is not Rasiowa–Sikorski doing work; that is what
"finite and total" means. `lem:bdd`'s countability payoff matters when
the dense family is genuinely infinite and you need a *construction*
that meets all of it without ever finishing; here there is nothing to
construct — you can just look. Compare the root: `r` itself, per chapter
04, does **not** decide b under `⊩_adm` — the lab reasserts this — even
though *every branch through r* decides it. The generic's job is exactly
to descend far enough to buy the decision; on a finite spine the
descent is three steps and always terminates in a leaf that already
knew everything. On an unbounded observer's actual dense family,
nothing guarantees a leaf, and that is precisely where `lem:reach`'s
conditionality and `lem:bdd`'s Rasiowa–Sikorski construction earn their
keep.

sections.title: Lab
sections.markdown:
```bash
python book/labs/lab05_generics.py
```

Checks the truth-lemma pattern over every branch and the small formula
set, checks bivalence holds on every branch, and reasserts (from chapter
04's kernel) that r remains undecided on b under `⊩_adm`.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.markdown:
1. **Predict, then run.** Add atom `c`, stabilized only at leaf `01`.
   Predict, for the branch `{r, 0, 01}`, whether `G ⊨ c` and which
   condition in `G` forces it; then extend the lab's formula list and
   check.
2. Explain in one sentence why a branch on *any* finite tree with a
   total leaf valuation must decide every formula in the propositional
   closure of the atoms — no appeal to Rasiowa–Sikorski required.
3. `lem:reach` requires the smoothness contract. Sketch (prose, not
   code) a modification of the spine's `J_adm` generator such that some
   deciding sieve stops being a `J_adm`-cover even though it stays
   order-dense — you are building a miniature failure of Decision
   Reachability. (You do not need to prove the general contract fails;
   exhibit the local symptom.)
4. (Harder.) `thm:prop`'s induction handles ∨ by routing through
   deciding sets rather than the naive "disjunct-forcers form a cover."
   Using the ¬¬b countermodel from chapter 04 (`rem:torsion`), explain
   why the naive routing would have broken exactly at a disjunction
   built from that compound.

sections.title: Boundary note
sections.markdown:
This chapter does **not** claim the lab demonstrates Rasiowa–Sikorski
constructing anything, or that Decision Reachability's conditionality is
exercised here — both require either a genuinely infinite dense family
or a site where the smoothness contract actually fails, and the
7-condition spine has neither. What the lab *does* verify: the
truth-lemma pattern holds exactly where the paper says it must, and the
undecided-root/decided-branch asymmetry that motivates the whole
apparatus is a real, computed fact, not a story. The substantive proofs
— that the construction terminates for genuinely unbounded families, and
what happens when the contract is violated — live in the paper's proof
environments and, for the base cases, in the Lean kernel
(`rasiowa_sikorski`, `truth_lemma`, `exists_generic_truth`); this chapter
is not a substitute for either.

# Chapter 05 · Generics and Truth

## Aim

After this chapter you can state Rasiowa–Sikorski genericity, say what
Decision Reachability adds beyond plain order-density, and explain why a
generic *decides every formula* (bivalence) without that fact being

content.

## Prerequisites

Decision Reachability introduced informally). This chapter states the
lemma that names it.

## Definitions

**Admissible deciding closure** `D_p*` (`def:closure`, D). The countable

## Claims

**Boundedness yields countability for Rasiowa–Sikorski** (`lem:bdd`,

(the observer-bound postulate M-Bound supplies *countability*, not
density); Rasiowa–Sikorski applied to a countable family of dense sets
produces a filter meeting all of them. This is where the classical

**Decision Reachability, conditional** (`lem:reach`, **P** consuming the

holds unconditionally, but admissible density is the contract's payoff,
and where the contract fails, the finite model checker exhibits sites

the calibration debt, not a consequence of it.

**Genericity decides every formula** (`lem:bivalence`, **P** consuming
C). A generic meeting `D_p*` decides every propositional formula — some

**Propositional Truth Lemma, conditional on calibration** (`thm:prop`,
**P** — `truth_lemma`/`exists_generic_truth` in the Lean kernel, under
the calibration queue `asm:calibration`). For a generic G deciding every
formula: `G ⊨_H φ ⟺ ∃p ∈ G : p ⊩_H φ`. Induction on φ; the atomic base
case is `lem:atomic`, whose (⇒) direction routes through the *maximal
sieve* — the v14 text's mistake was concluding this from density alone,
which does not imply `J_adm`-covering; only the maximal sieve, present
in every Grothendieck topology, does.

## Worked example: the truth lemma pattern, live — and its trivialization

The lab checks, over all 4 branches and 7 formulas (a, b, ¬a, ¬b, ¬¬b,
a∧b, a∨b): **`G ⊨ φ` iff some `q ∈ G` forces φ under `⊩_adm`.** Every one
of the 28 checks matches. That is a genuine, computed instance of
`thm:prop`'s pattern — not asserted, verified.

Now the honest part. Every branch on this model **decides every
formula, trivially** — bivalence never fails, in fact *cannot* fail,
because the valuation is total on the leaves: a leaf is either in `V(a)`
or it isn't, full stop, and a branch's classical truth is read straight
off its leaf. That is not Rasiowa–Sikorski doing work; that is what
"finite and total" means. `lem:bdd`'s countability payoff matters when
the dense family is genuinely infinite and you need a *construction*
that meets all of it without ever finishing; here there is nothing to
construct — you can just look. Compare the root: `r` itself, per chapter
04, does **not** decide b under `⊩_adm` — the lab reasserts this — even
though *every branch through r* decides it. The generic's job is exactly
to descend far enough to buy the decision; on a finite spine the
descent is three steps and always terminates in a leaf that already
knew everything. On an unbounded observer's actual dense family,
nothing guarantees a leaf, and that is precisely where `lem:reach`'s
conditionality and `lem:bdd`'s Rasiowa–Sikorski construction earn their
keep.

## Lab

```bash
python book/labs/lab05_generics.py
```

Checks the truth-lemma pattern over every branch and the small formula
set, checks bivalence holds on every branch, and reasserts (from chapter
04's kernel) that r remains undecided on b under `⊩_adm`.

## Exercises

1. **Predict, then run.** Add atom `c`, stabilized only at leaf `01`.
   Predict, for the branch `{r, 0, 01}`, whether `G ⊨ c` and which
   condition in `G` forces it; then extend the lab's formula list and
   check.
2. Explain in one sentence why a branch on *any* finite tree with a
   total leaf valuation must decide every formula in the propositional
   closure of the atoms — no appeal to Rasiowa–Sikorski required.
3. `lem:reach` requires the smoothness contract. Sketch (prose, not
   code) a modification of the spine's `J_adm` generator such that some
   deciding sieve stops being a `J_adm`-cover even though it stays
   order-dense — you are building a miniature failure of Decision
   Reachability. (You do not need to prove the general contract fails;
   exhibit the local symptom.)
4. (Harder.) `thm:prop`'s induction handles ∨ by routing through
   deciding sets rather than the naive "disjunct-forcers form a cover."
   Using the ¬¬b countermodel from chapter 04 (`rem:torsion`), explain
   why the naive routing would have broken exactly at a disjunction
   built from that compound.

## Boundary note

This chapter does **not** claim the lab demonstrates Rasiowa–Sikorski
constructing anything, or that Decision Reachability's conditionality is
exercised here — both require either a genuinely infinite dense family
or a site where the smoothness contract actually fails, and the
7-condition spine has neither. What the lab *does* verify: the
truth-lemma pattern holds exactly where the paper says it must, and the
undecided-root/decided-branch asymmetry that motivates the whole
apparatus is a real, computed fact, not a story. The substantive proofs
— that the construction terminates for genuinely unbounded families, and
what happens when the contract is violated — live in the paper's proof
environments and, for the base cases, in the Lean kernel
(`rasiowa_sikorski`, `truth_lemma`, `exists_generic_truth`); this chapter
is not a substitute for either.

prerequisites.title: Forcing: When Does a Claim Hold?
